figures.caption: Table 1: Task-driven representation learning under the unified formulation. Each method is specified by an observed view X̃ , a target signal T (X), and a task loss Ltask. Here M⌝ denotes the visible complement of the masked set M , N− denotes the set of negative candidates for sample X , s(·, ·) is a similarity function, such as the cosine similarity, and q(X) denotes a cluster or prototype assignment.

Table 2: Representation learning with generic latent constraints. These methods supplement a task component with a non-causal constraint Ωgen that shapes the latent space through compression, prior alignment, sparsity, energy-based preferences, or discreteness. qθ denotes a stochastic encoder, p denotes a reference distribution, DKL(·∥·) denotes KL divergence, Eψ denotes an energy function, and {ek}Kk=1 denotes a finite codebook.

Table 3: Representative causal constraints under the unified formulation. U denotes the auxiliary information, such as the label, domain indicator, and temporal indicator, to provide sufficient observations. The pair (v1, v2) can be the observations from different views, or with/without interventions. d(·, ·) measures discrepancy. In the intervention case, DeG(I) denotes the descendants in the causal graph G under intervention I . g(k)ϕ (ZBk ) denotes the sub-decoder that generates part of the observations with a latent block, and α(k) ϕ (Z) denotes the corresponding weight, with ∑K k=1 α (k) ϕ (Z) = 1.

Figure 1: Comparison of temporal dynamics with and without instantaneous relations. Dashed arrows denote the generation function g∗, and solid arrows denote temporal transitions governed by m. (a) Without instantaneous relations, the latent variables at time t depend only on variables from earlier time steps. (b) With instantaneous relations, latent variables at the same time step can additionally influence each other (blue arrows).
